title: Giant orbital-magnon conversion driven perpendicular magnetization switching
authors: Fanyu Meng, Ying Feng, Mingyang Sun, Baiyan Kang, Donglin Song, Tuo Zhang, Jia Zhang, Wenping Zhou, Jijun Zhao, Yi Wang
abstract: The pursuit of beyond-Moore information technologies has stimulated the exploration of novel information carriers, such as electron spin, orbital, and magnon, beyond electron charge. Efficient interconversion among these degrees of freedom and precise control over the information states are crucial for advancing nanoelectronic devices. However, a direct coupling between orbital angular momentum (L) and magnons (M) has remained elusive, and magnetization switching through orbital-to-magnon (L-M) conversion has not yet been achieved. Here, we report the experimental demonstration of L-M conversion in an orbital metal/antiferromagnetic insulator bilayer at room temperature, with an efficiency over an order of magnitude higher than that in traditional orbital systems lacking the L-M process. Consequently, we achieved efficient room-temperature perpendicular magnetization switching in a CoFeB ferromagnetic layer mediated by this new mechanism. Our findings establish a direct link between orbitronics and magnonics, providing a new platform for the development of advanced nano-devices based on orbital-driven magnonic phenomena.
summary: The researchers demonstrated a new way to control magnetism by converting orbital angular momentum into magnons within a NiO insulator. This process is much more efficient than previous methods, offering a promising path for developing low-power, high-speed nanoelectronic devices.
main_problem: The difficulty in achieving efficient magnetization switching in orbital-based heterostructures due to low orbital-to-electron spin conversion and the lack of a demonstrated direct coupling between orbital angular momentum and magnons.
main_result: Experimental demonstration of efficient orbital-to-magnon (L-M) conversion in a Ti/NiO/CoFeB bilayer at room temperature, achieving magnetization switching with efficiency significantly higher than traditional orbital or spin-only systems.
method: Spin-Torque Ferromagnetic Resonance (ST-FMR), second-harmonic Hall voltage measurements, MOKE microscopy, and temperature-dependent transport measurements.
model_or_system: An orbital metal/antiferromagnetic insulator bilayer system, specifically Ti/NiO/CoFeB and W/NiO/Ti/CoFeB multilayer stacks, utilizing Ti as an orbital current source and NiO as the medium for L-M conversion.
key_observables: Damping-like torque efficiency (theta_DL), orbital-to-magnon conversion coefficient (C_MI), orbital Hall conductivity, and switching efficiency (eta).
important_parameters: NiO thickness (t_NiO), Néel temperature (T_N), orbital diffusion length (lambda_O), and magnon diffusion length (lambda_M).
figures_summary: Fig 1: Schematic of L-M conversion mechanism. Fig 2: ST-FMR signals, theta_DL dependence on NiO thickness and temperature, and exchange bias. Fig 3: Comparison of L-M conversion coefficient in NiO vs. other metals. Fig 4: Demonstration of current-induced magnetization switching.
paper_structure: Introduction of the L-M conversion problem, experimental setup and material characterization, demonstration of thickness and temperature-dependent torque, comparison of conversion efficiencies, and demonstration of efficient magnetization switching.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
